fact: June 3 is exactly 154 days into the year, which means there are exactly 211 days left until New Year's Eve!
In the Northern Hemisphere, the sun rises super early around June 3 - sometimes before 5:30 AM in places like New York!
Fireflies typically start their magical blinking shows around this time in North America, turning backyards into twinkling fairy lands.
June 3 falls in the middle of Gemini season in the zodiac calendar.
In Australia and other Southern Hemisphere places, June 3 marks the beginning of winter, so kids might be building snowmen while American kids are swimming!
The sun sets after 8 PM in many northern cities around June 3, giving kids extra daylight hours to play outside after dinner.
Cherry trees in many parts of the world have usually finished blooming by June 3, leaving behind tiny green cherries that will turn red and juicy by summer's end.
June 3 sits just 18 days before the summer solstice, making it one of the longest days of the year in terms of daylight hours.
Baby deer called fawns are often wobbling around on their skinny legs for the first time around June 3 in forests across North America.
In northern Alaska, the sun barely sets at all around June 3 - it just dips toward the horizon and pops right back up like a yo-yo!
Dandelions have typically transformed from bright yellow flowers into fluffy white puff balls by June 3, ready for kids to blow and make wishes.
